data.title: R.J. O'Brien to merge with StoneX Group
data.description:
• R.J. O'Brien & Associates has reached a definitive agreement with StoneX Group (NASDAQ:SNEX), to merge all of its global businesses into StoneX, the company said on Monday.
  • the transaction inexpected to close in the third quarter of this year.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Condor Resources looks to raise C$1.5M in private placement
data.description:
• Condor Resources (CN:CA) said it is looking to raise C$1.5M in a private placement of up to 12.5M units at a price of C$0.12 apiece.
  • Each unit will have one and one-half of one non-transferable common share purchase warrant.
  • Each whole warrant will be exercisable for a period of 36 months from the closing date and will entitle the holder thereof to purchase one additional common share.
  • The exercise price will be C$0.15 per warrant share if duly exercised on or before the date that is 24 months following the closing date, or thereafter at an exercise price of C$0.20 apiece.
  • The firm expects to use the net proceeds raised for exploration at the Huinac Punta Project, the Pucamayo Project, and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: Best Buy soars amid confusion concerning tariffs on electronics
data.description:
Best Buy (NYSE:BBY) rallied in early trading on Monday after the Trump administration exempted smartphones, computers, and other electronics mostly imported from China from reciprocal tariffs to provide major relief for American tech players.

The new U.S. Customs and Border Protection guidance was issued late Friday. However, President Trump and his Commerce secretary, Howard Lutnick, then suggested that the exemptions were not permanent, which has led to some uncertainty on the path ahead, according to analysts tasked with projecting earnings.

"Over the weekend, Trump partially retreated from the retreat, emphasizing that a 20% tax would apply and additional unspecified taxes on technology are coming. This erratic policymaking leads to investors questioning the existence of a competent plan," warned UBS analyst Paul Donovan.

For Best Buy (NYSE:BBY) investors, the wild swings could continue as trade deals are worked out. Shares of Best Buy (BBY) were up 8.1% in premarket action on Monday to follow a 1.1% gain on Friday. The consumer electronics stock is still down about 24% on a year-to-date basis. Short interest on BBY stands at 6.7% of the total float.

data.title: Better Home & Finance shares rise as it retires approximately $530M convertible notes
data.description:
• Better Home & Finance (NASDAQ:BETR) said on Monday that it has restructured its outstanding convertible notes with its lender to retire approximately $530M of outstanding debt in exchange for a one-time cash consideration of $110M and the issuance of $155M of new senior secured notes. 
  • The new senior secured notes are from SB Northstar LP, the company’s existing noteholder, which will be due December 31, 2028, and will accrue interest at a rate of 6% per annum that is payable, at Better’s option, in kind or in cash.
  • The company expects creation of approximately $265M of pre-tax equity, excluding discounts on the debt.
  • Shares of Better Home & Finance (NASDAQ:BETR) were up more than 16% in pre-market trading.

data.title: Goldman Sachs in charts: Q1 Global Banking & Markets +26% Q/Q, AWM sales -22%, NII +80% Y/Y
Earnings Snapshot: Goldman Sachs surpasses Q1 estimates, equities trading revenue beat
Danaos upsizes its share repurchase program by an additional $100M
data.description:
• Danaos Corporation (NYSE:DAC) said on Monday that its board has approved the upsizing of its common stock repurchase program by an additional $100M.
  • The company has already executed on share repurchases of approximately $196M out of the $200M existing share repurchase program that is now being upsized to a total of $300M.
  • Danaos Corporation (NYSE:DAC) shares were up 0.99% in pre-market trading.

data.title: Apple takes top spot in global smartphone market in Q1 - Counterpoint
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone took the number top spot in the first quarter of 2025, its first ever for the first quarter, with a 19% share in the global smartphone market, according to Counterpoint Research.

The U.S. tech giant took the number one place spot in the first quarter despite the challenges faced in its biggest markets. Sales in the U.S., Europe and China were either flat or declining, but Apple saw double-digit growth in Japan, India, Middle East and Africa, and Southeast Asia, the report added.

The iPhone saw a +4% year-over-year unit change in the first quarter of 2025.

South Korea's Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) came in second with an 18% market share. The researchers noted that the company had a slow start due to the late launch of the S25 series but sales showed a resurgence after the launch of the flagship S25 and new A-series devices.

Samsung’s sales grew by double-digits in March. The share of “Ultra” in the S25 series sales showed an increase. Samsung recorded a -5% year-over-year unit change in the first quarter of 2025, according to the report.

"Sales in January were particularly strong, with a subsidy-led demand boost in China. The momentum continued with major launches like Samsung’s S25 and iPhone 16e, but it turned quickly as economic uncertainties and trade war risks started mounting, especially towards the end of the quarter," said Senior Research Analyst Ankit Malhotra.

Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) (OTCPK:XIACY) took the third spot with a 14% market share. The researchers said Xiaomi continued its strong sales momentum and gained market share. The company was helped by expansion into new markets and significant growth in the domestic market, where the brand is seeing "a bigger and more ‘premium’ brand presence thanks to its successful foray into electric vehicles," the report added.

The fastest-growing top-five brand, vivo, gained a rank to take the fourth place, helped by high exposure to the China market and expansion into emerging markets.

Meanwhile, OPPO rounded off the top five, and saw sales growth in India, Latin America and Europe, as per the report.

The global smartphone market grew 3%  year-over-year in the first quarter of 2025, according to preliminary results from Counterpoint.

The declines in developed markets were offset by growth in China owing to government subsidies, and continuing recovery in key emerging markets across Latin America, Asia-Pacific and Middle East and Africa.

"The market got off to a mixed start in 2025, where Q1 saw continued improvement in economic conditions, particularly in emerging markets. But mature markets like North America, Europe and China showed signs of fatigue after a recovery in 2024," said Malhotra.

Malhotra added that they have revised their full-year forecast and now expect volumes to decline slightly year-over-year in 2025, despite growth in the first quarter.

data.title: Nvidia, Broadcom still among BofA's top chip picks after tariff delay
data.description:
Following the Trump administration's decision to exempt phones, semiconductors and computers from additional tariffs, Bank of America has reiterated its top semiconductor stocks to own, a list that includes Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA).

“Even though electronics tariffs have been delayed and not necessarily canceled, we view the 1-2 month pause as a net positive as it allows for debate/discussion on this critical end-market that contributes ~50% of all chip demand,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients.

The analysts added that the comments from the Trump administration now appear to be focusing on boosting the U.S.'s self-sufficiency in semiconductor production, which they said is a “related but different goal than implementing tariffs to close persistent trade gaps.” And with President Trump clarifying that new “sectoral” tariffs are coming in the next couple of months, that could wind up impacting U.S. electronics that are assembled in China and use processors that are made overseas, the analysts posited.

“We don’t know what these new tariff rates could be but (optimistically) assume they could give due consideration to recent announcements on enhanced US investments from TSMC ($195bn), Apple ($500bn) and Nvidia ($200bn.),” the analysts explained. “We expect over time for [Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM)] to front-end process most if not all semis required by its US fabless customers within its fabs in Arizona.”  

Delving deeper, the analysts said that artificial intelligence-related semiconductor companies, like Nvidia and Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) should benefit, given the “resilient” nature of cloud capex spending. They also like chip design firms, such as Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ:CDNS) and Synopsys (SNPS), both of which have “resilient” R&D spending. 

They also mentioned Lam Research (NASDAQ:LRCX) and KLA Corp. (KLAC) as two of the semiconductor equipment firms that are likely to benefit from reshoring, and both remain “well positioned to largely deliver on earnings expectations.”

data.title: China seeks to boost Southeast Asian ties as Trump tariffs loom
data.description:
As Donald Trump's turbulent tariff moves rattle global markets, Chinese President Xi Jinping is expected to use his first overseas trip of the year to present China as a more stable trade partner.

Xi is visiting three Southeast Asian countries this week, starting with Vietnam on Monday, followed by Malaysia and Cambodia. Recall that once the 90-day pause on Trump's reciprocal tariffs ends, Vietnam is set to face a 46% tariff rate, Malaysia 24%, and Cambodia 49%.

"We should work together with the Global South to uphold the common interests of developing countries," Xi wrote in an article published in Nhan Dan (official newspaper of Vietnam's Communist Party).

"The trade war will produce no winner, and protectionism will lead nowhere. Our countries should resolutely safeguard the multilateral trading system, stable global industrial and supply chains, and open international environment," he added.

China is a major trading partner of Vietnam, Malaysia and Cambodia. But these countries are seeking exemptions from U.S. President Donald Trump's steep tariffs, for which his administration has laid out clear terms. 

"We will want to hear from countries including Cambodia, Mexico and Vietnam that you will stop allowing China to evade U.S. tariffs by trans-shipping exports through your countries," Trump's key adviser Peter Navarro wrote in a recent op-ed published in the Financial Times. 

To note, Vietnam and Malaysia have territorial disputes with Beijing in the South China Sea. China is unlikely to compromise on these disputes, but it may pledge more investment and trade to strengthen ties.

Cambodia, which has close ties with China and relies heavily on Chinese investment, is facing the highest tariff rate in Trump's reciprocal tariff list.

China also aims to reach out to the European Union as part of its diplomatic strategy to counter Trump's moves to isolate the country. The bloc's leaders are scheduled to meet with Xi in Beijing in July, at a time when the EU's ties with Washington are strained.
